programs.name: DenaliCare
programs.what_it_does: DenaliCare is Alaska's Medicaid program that provides health coverage for seniors and individuals with disabilities. It covers limited expenses in senior living facilities and other medical needs.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 65+ and disabled individuals with low income meeting Medicaid eligibility, including U.S. citizens or qualified aliens residing in Alaska.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by eligibility; covers premiums, deductibles, coinsurance for qualifying services.[4]

programs.name: Adults with Physical & Developmental Disabilities Waiver (APDD)
programs.what_it_does: The APDD waiver provides home and community-based services to help eligible adults avoid nursing home placement. Services include home modifications and personal care assistance.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Adults over 21 with physical or developmental disabilities requiring nursing home level of care, including eligible seniors.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Varies; covers home modifications, care services tailored to needs.[4]

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: PACE offers comprehensive medical and social services to help frail seniors stay in the community. It coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid covered care plus additional services.
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 55+ who are nursing home eligible, certified by the state, and live in a PACE service area.
programs.benefit_value: Full coverage of approved services including adult day health, transportation, home care; no additional premiums for most.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: These programs help pay Medicare Part A/B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copays. Alaska has higher income limits than the contiguous 48 states.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries aged 65+; income limits higher in Alaska (e.g., tied to federal poverty levels).[4]
programs.benefit_value: Full/partial coverage of premiums (e.g., QMB covers Part A/B premiums, deductibles); varies by program.[4]

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: SNAP provides monthly benefits via EBT card for purchasing food. Seniors 60+ get deductions for medical expenses over $35/month and simplified application.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ with household income up to $1,580/month (individual).[3]

programs.name: Alaska Energy Assistance (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides grants to help with heating costs during winter. Application period runs October to March.[3]

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Funds home energy efficiency improvements like insulation and sealing to reduce heating costs. Available through state and federal partnerships.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households, prioritizing seniors and disabled.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Free or low-cost home upgrades; value varies by home needs.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers free counseling on Medicare, Medigap, Medicare Advantage, and prescription drug plans. Helps with plan selection, appeals, and cost-saving options.
programs.who_its_for: All Medicare beneficiaries, especially seniors in Alaska.
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling and group education sessions.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (Alaska Senior Nutrition Programs)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers hot nutritious meals to homebound seniors and provides congregate meals at centers. Supports independence and nutrition.
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals; congregate for active 60+.
programs.benefit_value: 1-2 meals/day delivered; suggested donation-based for low-income.

programs.name: Alzheimer's Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides respite care, counseling, and training for caregivers of those with Alzheimer's or related dementias. Expands access to caregiver support services.
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of seniors 60+ with Alzheimer's/related disorders.
programs.benefit_value: Respite grants up to program limits; training and support services.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time community service jobs with training to unemployed low-income seniors. Helps transition to unsubsidized employment.
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage pay for 20 hours/week; work-based training.

programs.name: Alaska Legal Services Corporation (Senior Legal Aid)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free civil legal assistance to low-income seniors. Covers issues like housing, benefits, consumer problems, and elder abuse.
programs.benefit_value: Free legal advice, representation, and self-help resources.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, investigating complaints and ensuring quality care. Provides education and resolves issues confidentially.
programs.who_its_for: Residents of nursing homes, assisted living, and similar facilities in Alaska.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy, complaint resolution, and rights protection services.

programs.name: Senior Benefits Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly cash benefits to eligible Alaska residents aged 65 or older with low to moderate income. Payment levels are $125, $175, or $250 depending on income tied to Alaska Federal Poverty Guidelines, subject to available state funding.
programs.who_its_for: Alaska residents age 65+, U.S. citizens or qualified immigrants, with gross annual income not exceeding program limits (e.g., under $11,963 for single applicants eligible for $250/month).
programs.whats_unique: State-funded cash assistance program unique to Alaska, evolved from the discontinued Alaska Longevity Bonus; not available in other states.
programs.benefit_value: $76-$250 per month based on income and funding

programs.name: Adult Public Assistance (APA)
programs.what_it_does: Provides cash assistance to needy aged, blind, or disabled Alaskans to help them remain independent. Recipients must pursue all other available benefits like SSI or veterans' compensation.
programs.who_its_for: Alaska residents aged 65+ (or 18+ if blind/disabled), U.S. citizens or qualified immigrants, with resources ≤$2,000 (individual) or $3,000 (couple) and income within limits.
programs.whats_unique: Alaska's state-funded cash program supplementing federal SSI for seniors and disabled, with specific resource caps and mandatory pursuit of other benefits; not a standard federal program.
programs.benefit_value: Varies by individual needs and income; cash payments to SSI recipients and others

programs.name: Senior Access Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides funds for home accessibility modifications to help senior citizens over 55 live independently in the community. Funding availability varies and must be checked directly.
programs.who_its_for: Alaska seniors over age 55 needing home modifications for independent living.
programs.whats_unique: State-specific program funding home adaptations exclusively for Alaska seniors; not a nationwide offering.
programs.official_url: Not specified in sources; contact via Alaska 211 or local DPA
programs.benefit_value: Funds for accessibility modifications (amounts depend on availability)
